📐 Calibration — does "70% confident" actually win ~70%?
well-calibrated mild drift overconfident underconfident (high value)
Dashed line = perfect calibration. Above it = model underconfident in that range; below = overconfident. Dot size = sample size.
🔍 How the model works▶
Base Rating
📊
Elo + Stats Blend
Blends Elo (built from recorded fight history, when it exists) with a stats-based rating from career win rate, finish rate, experience, and reach.
Fighter-Specific Adjustments
🥋
Style Matchup — "Styles Make Fights"
Takedown accuracy vs. the opponent's takedown defense (or control time percentage, when populated — a fighter who goes 1-for-5 on takedowns but holds 4 minutes of control is more effective than raw accuracy implies), striking accuracy and volume differential (SLpM minus SApM, when populated), and durability all shift the base rating gap.
📉
Age Cliff by Weight Class
The decline threshold varies a lot by division — Flyweight/Bantamweight/Featherweight/Strawweight start seeing a real decline past 35, while Heavyweight and Light Heavyweight often sustain into the late 30s. A flat age penalty across all divisions would be wrong in both directions.
⏱️
Layoff / Ring Rust vs. Quick Return
Fighters back after more than a year away take a ring-rust penalty; fighters back too soon after being finished (under ~6 months) take a separate, opposite-direction penalty. The layoff weight was revised down sharply after backtesting against 30 years of fight history showed the original penalty was sized far too aggressively — the current weight is the strongest the historical evidence actually supports.
🔥
Recent Form (Backtest-Validated)
Each fighter's last three results, weighted by how recent they are (a win last month counts more than a win 18 months ago). Both the three-fight window and the weight were selected on pre-2019 historical fights and confirmed on held-out 2019+ fights — one of the few adjustments fitted to evidence rather than hand-tuned.
🤜
Stance Matchup
Southpaw or switch fighters get a modest edge against pure-orthodox opponents — the classic "unfamiliar look" advantage. Two fighters sharing a stance (including two southpaws) is neutral, since neither has the familiarity edge.
🐍
Submission Threat
A fighter's rate of finishing wins by submission, as a distinct skill from raw wrestling stats — a fighter can have modest takedown numbers but a live submission threat off scrambles and clinch entries, which takedown accuracy alone misses.
🔀
Weight Class Change
Moving up a division (facing bigger opponents) carries a penalty per division jumped; moving down earns a small bonus. Detected automatically from each fighter's tracked division history against the division this specific fight is booked at.
📏
Height Advantage
A small edge per inch of height difference — deliberately small, because reach (which tracks height closely) already carries weight in the base rating, and counting the same physical edge twice would overstate it.
🚨
Short Notice
Fighters who accept a bout on short notice — a late replacement without a full training camp — win measurably less often; published analyses of UFC short-notice outcomes put the drop at roughly 10 percentage points, which is the penalty applied here. Flagged per fighter when a late booking is confirmed, and cleared automatically once that fight happens, so a one-booking fact never bleeds into the fighter's next matchup.
⚖️
Missed Weight
A documented red flag for a rushed or broken camp — factored in when known, though real weigh-in history isn't fully populated for the roster yet (see limitations below).
Prop Predictions
🎯
Method of Victory
Prior-informed blend — starts at the divisional average rate for that method (heavyweight fights have an inherently higher baseline KO/TKO rate than strawweight, which leans toward decisions), shifts toward the fighter's own career tendency (weighted by experience), then further incorporates the specific opponent's demonstrated vulnerability.
🔔
Total Rounds
Blends combined finish rate with first-round finish percentage. Main events get 5-round-calibrated lines (3.5/4.5) instead of 3-round lines (1.5/2.5), since there's simply more scheduled fight to cover.
Market & Betting Math
📈
Line Movement
Each refresh is compared against saved history to show real price movement and render an actual chart. Honest limitation: this tracks price only, not bet volume or who's behind a move — true "sharp money" detection needs handle/ticket-count data no free source provides, so this is labeled "notable movement," not a sharp-money claim.
💰
Suggested Stake
Tenth-Kelly, hard-capped at 5% of bankroll — and sized from a probability that's deliberately shrunk most of the way toward the market's own de-vigged price, not the raw model number. Backtesting showed the model's standalone probabilities carry real but modest signal, while sportsbook closing lines are among the sharpest predictors in sports — so when the model and the market disagree hugely, the stake respects that the market is usually the one that's right. Practical effect: big claimed edges get meaningfully smaller stakes than raw Kelly would suggest, and small edges (under roughly 5 points) stake zero, because after the shrink they no longer clear the vig. That's the system being deliberately humble, not broken.
🎢
Closing Line Value (CLV)
The moneyline price on the model's favorite the FIRST time it was logged, compared against the last known price before the fight. If the market moved toward the model's side by fight night (the price shortened), that's a "beat" — a real signal the model saw value before the market caught up, independent of whether the pick actually won. This is why CLV is tracked separately from raw accuracy: a model can lose a coinflip fight while still having correctly read where the market was headed, and that skill is what CLV isolates.
🔬
Historical Upset Analysis
Separately from live tracking, the rating engine is walked back through its full fight history in chronological order — replaying each fight using only the ratings as they stood the moment before, no future information — to see how favorites at each confidence level actually lost when they lost: close decision, or getting finished. This is diagnostic, not automatic: a pattern here is a reason to investigate a specific adjustment, not an instruction that silently reweights anything on its own.
Validation
🧪
Walk-Forward Backtest
The rating backbone is validated against 30 years of real fight history — nearly 3,000 strictly out-of-sample predictions, each made only from fights that happened before it, never retrodicted. This is also what keeps the adjustment weights honest: it's how the layoff penalty got cut to a third of its original size, how the recent-form window was fitted rather than guessed, and how a proposed strength-of-schedule factor got rejected entirely (it looked good in-sample and fell apart on held-out data — the exact failure the backtest exists to catch). Factors that can't be validated this way, like short notice, are labeled by what actually backs them instead of being presented as fitted.
⚠️ Honest data limitations: strike-volume (SLpM/SApM), control-time percentage, and missed-weight history are new schema fields the model is built to use, but they're not populated for the full roster yet — those specific adjustments quietly no-op (rather than guess) until real data backs them. Everything else above is live and active.

Mitch Ramirez (vs. Chase Hooper)

Mitch Ramirez to win by KO/TKO is priced at +590 (14% implied), while the model estimates 55% (+40.7% edge). Chase Hooper has gone down by KO/TKO in 60% of their career losses -- a real, specific vulnerability this matchup plays into, on top of Mitch Ramirez's own 62% career rate finishing fights that way.

Favorite Picks
Straight, single-leg bets in a realistic odds range (-220 to +160) — built for real size, not a coinflip longshot or a -4000 favorite with no payout. Sorted by model probability, so the safest genuine plays lead.
Tommy McMillen
vs Alberto Montes · Moneyline
-153
79.8% model confidence
On the feet, Tommy McMillen lands at a clip Alberto Montes hasn't shown much answer for -- that's the kind of advantage that tends to compound over a full fight rather than fade. At -153, that's real, bettable value on a pick the model has at 80% -- the kind of number worth sizing up on rather than treating as a coinflip.
Kamaru Usman vs Dricus Du Plessis
Fight Outcome: Ends In Finish
+108
67.3% model confidence
Fight Outcome: Ends In Finish at +108 implies 48%, vs. the model's 67% (+19.3% edge). Dricus Du Plessis rarely sees a decision (13% of career wins), a sharp contrast to Kamaru Usman's 52%. That's the engine behind an early-finish lean.
Locks of the Week
The model's single highest-conviction picks on this weekend's card, all in one place — previously only visible as a badge you'd have to find on each individual fight. Capped at 3 per event, so a real standout doesn't get diluted by padded-out picks.
Tommy McMillen vs. Alberto Montes
Featherweight · Main Card · by KO/TKO
Model favors Tommy McMillen at 80% over Alberto Montes (Striker vs. Striker stylistically). Path to victory most likely runs through ko/tko (projected at 58%, weighing McMillen's own tendencies against Montes's specific vulnerability profile). Combined finish rate between both fighters sits at 78%, leaning under on total rounds. Tommy McMillen has been finished less often historically, a durability factor working in their favor. Tommy McMillen also holds a notable reach advantage (5 inches). Tommy McMillen is a genuine round-1 threat — 90% of their career wins have come before the first round even ends, which should pull any rounds/distance line lower regardless of who's favored to win outright. Note: the situational factors here stack unusually high, hitting the model's sanity cap -- the final number is deliberately more conservative than the raw factor pile would suggest.
Alden Coria vs. Stewart Nicoll
Flyweight · Prelims · by KO/TKO
Model favors Alden Coria at 77% over Stewart Nicoll (Wrestler/Grappler vs. Balanced stylistically). Path to victory most likely runs through ko/tko (projected at 39%, weighing Coria's own tendencies against Nicoll's specific vulnerability profile). Combined finish rate between both fighters sits at 81%, leaning under on total rounds. Alden Coria's takedown accuracy against the opponent's takedown defense gives a real wrestling-based edge here. Stewart Nicoll is coming back quickly after being finished by KO/TKO in their last fight — a short turnaround from a finish carries real risk that career numbers alone won't show. Note: the situational factors here stack unusually high, hitting the model's sanity cap -- the final number is deliberately more conservative than the raw factor pile would suggest.

Sat, Jul 18

Kamaru Usman

🏆 Kamaru Usman by Decision Low Confidence
🧠 Model Preview: Model favors Kamaru Usman at 51% over Dricus Du Plessis (Wrestler/Grappler vs. Wrestler/Grappler stylistically). Path to victory most likely runs through decision (projected at 47%, weighing Usman's own tendencies against Plessis's specific vulnerability profile). Combined finish rate between both fighters sits at 67%, leaning under on total rounds. Kamaru Usman has been finished less often historically, a durability factor working in their favor. Kamaru Usman is returning from a 1.1-year layoff, which carries real ring-rust risk regardless of what their career numbers say. Kamaru Usman (38) is past the typical age cliff for Middleweight — this division tends to see a real decline past that point, independent of career record. This is scheduled for 5 rounds, not the usual 3 — cardio and championship rounds matter here.

Kamaru Usman by KO/TKO: Kamaru Usman to win by KO/TKO is priced at +545 (16% implied), while the model estimates 40% (+24.5% edge). No single factor dominates here -- it's a blend of Kamaru Usman's own 43% career rate by KO/TKO and how Dricus Du Plessis has historically fared against that specific type of finish.
Dricus Du Plessis by KO/TKO: Dricus Du Plessis to win by KO/TKO is priced at +212 (32% implied), while the model estimates 52% (+19.5% edge). Kamaru Usman has gone down by KO/TKO in 50% of their career losses -- a real, specific vulnerability this matchup plays into, on top of Dricus Du Plessis's own 52% career rate finishing fights that way.
Kamaru Usman vs Dricus Du Plessis — Fight Outcome: Ends In Finish: Fight Outcome: Ends In Finish at +108 implies 48%, vs. the model's 67% (+19.3% edge). Dricus Du Plessis rarely sees a decision (13% of career wins), a sharp contrast to Kamaru Usman's 52%. That's the engine behind an early-finish lean.
Kamaru Usman vs Dricus Du Plessis — Fight Outcome: Goes The Distance: Fight Outcome: Goes The Distance at -108 implies 52%, vs. the model's 33% (-19.3% edge). Split profile here -- Kamaru Usman goes to the cards 52% of the time, but Dricus Du Plessis only 13%. Going the distance really hinges on Dricus Du Plessis's usual finishing instinct not showing up.
Kamaru Usman — Moneyline: The model puts Kamaru Usman's win probability at 52%, higher than the market's 34% implied probability at +190 (+17.1% edge). Biggest factors in that number: Kamaru Usman having been finished less often historically, Dricus Du Plessis's real submission-finish rate, Kamaru Usman coming off a 1.1-year layoff (ring rust risk).
Dricus Du Plessis — Moneyline: The model puts Dricus Du Plessis's win probability at 48%, lower than the market's 66% implied probability at -190 (-17.1% edge). Biggest factors in that number: Kamaru Usman having been finished less often historically, Dricus Du Plessis's real submission-finish rate, Kamaru Usman coming off a 1.1-year layoff (ring rust risk).

Combined finish rate between both fighters sits at 26%, leaning over on total rounds. Bogdan Guskov has been finished less often historically, a durability factor working in their favor.
